title.zh: 卷四十 張陳王周傳
title.en: Volume 40: Zhang, Chen, Wang and Zhou
title.raw: 卷四十 張陳王周傳 Volume 40: Zhang, Chen, Wang and Zhou

sentences.zh: 張良字子房，其先韓人也。

translations.literal: Zhang Liang, courtesy name Zifang, was by ancestry a man of Han.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Liang, whose courtesy name was Zifang, came from the old state of Han.

sentences.zh: 大父開地，相韓昭侯、宣惠王、襄哀王。

translations.literal: His grandfather Kaidi served as chancellor to Marquis Zhao of Han, King Xuanhui, and King Xiang'ai.
translations.idiomatic: His grandfather Kaidi had been chancellor under Han's Marquis Zhao, King Xuanhui, and King Xiang'ai.

sentences.zh: 父平，相釐王、悼惠王。

translations.literal: His father Ping served as chancellor to King Xi and King Daohui.
translations.idiomatic: His father Ping served King Xi and King Daohui as their chancellor.

sentences.zh: 悼惠王二十三年，平卒。

translations.literal: In the twenty-third year of King Daohui, Ping died.
translations.idiomatic: Ping died in the twenty-third year of King Daohui's reign.

sentences.zh: 卒二十歲，秦滅韓。

translations.literal: Twenty years after his death, Qin destroyed Han.
translations.idiomatic: Two decades later, Qin conquered Han.

sentences.zh: 良年少，未宦事韓。

translations.literal: Liang was still young and had not yet taken office to serve Han.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Liang was young and had not yet entered government service for Han.

sentences.zh: 韓破，良家僮三百人，弟死不葬，悉以家財求客刺秦王，為韓報仇，以五世相韓故。

translations.literal: When Han fell, Liang had three hundred household retainers. He did not bury his younger brother but used his entire family fortune to seek retainers to assassinate the King of Qin, to avenge Han on account of five generations having served Han as chancellors.
translations.idiomatic: After Han fell, Zhang Liang kept three hundred retainers in his household. He left his younger brother unburied and spent his whole fortune hiring agents to kill the Qin ruler and avenge Han—because for five generations his forebears had served Han as chancellors.

sentences.zh: 良嘗學禮淮陽，東見倉海君，得力士，為鐵椎重百二十斤。

translations.literal: Liang once studied ritual in Huaiyang; eastward he met the Lord of Canghai and obtained a strong man; he made an iron hammer weighing one hundred twenty jin.
translations.idiomatic: He had studied the rites at Huaiyang, then traveled east and met the Lord of Canghai, from whom he recruited a brawny accomplice and had forged an iron hammer weighing a hundred and twenty jin.

sentences.zh: 秦皇帝東游，至博狼沙中，良與客狙擊秦皇帝，誤中副車。

translations.literal: The Qin emperor toured east and, when he reached Bolangsha, Liang and his guest ambushed the Qin emperor but mistakenly struck the attendant carriage.
translations.idiomatic: When the First Emperor of Qin toured the east and reached Bolangsha, Zhang Liang and his man tried to kill him but hit a decoy carriage by mistake.

sentences.zh: 秦皇帝大怒，大索天下，求賊急甚。

translations.literal: The Qin emperor was greatly enraged and searched the whole empire urgently for the culprits.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor flew into a rage and ordered a frantic empire-wide manhunt.

sentences.zh: 良乃更名姓，亡匿下邳。

translations.literal: Liang then changed his name and surname and fled into hiding at Xiapi.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Liang changed his identity and went underground at Xiapi.

sentences.zh: 良嘗閒從容步游下邳圯上，有一老父，衣褐，至良所，直墮其履圯下，顧謂良曰：「孺子下取履！」

translations.literal: Once, when Liang was strolling at leisure on a bridge at Xiapi, there was an old man in coarse clothes who came to where Liang was, deliberately dropped his shoe beneath the bridge, looked back and said to Liang, "Boy, go down and fetch my shoe!"
translations.idiomatic: One day he was walking idly on a bridge at Xiapi when an old man in a rough cloak came up, deliberately dropped his sandal under the bridge, turned to him and said, "Young man, go get my shoe."

sentences.zh: 良愕然，欲歐之。

translations.literal: Liang was startled and wanted to strike him.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Liang was so taken aback that he almost hit the old man.

sentences.zh: 為其老，乃彊忍，下取履，因跪進。

translations.literal: On account of his age, he forced himself to endure, went down to fetch the shoe, and then knelt to present it.
translations.idiomatic: Out of respect for his age he swallowed his anger, retrieved the shoe, and offered it back on his knees.

sentences.zh: 父以足受之，笑去。

translations.literal: The old man received it with his foot, laughed, and left.
translations.idiomatic: The old man stuck out his foot for him to put it on, chuckled, and walked away.

sentences.zh: 良殊大驚。

translations.literal: Liang was greatly astonished.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Liang was dumbfounded.

sentences.zh: 父去里所，復還，曰：「孺子可教矣。

translations.literal: The old man had gone about a li when he returned and said, "This boy can be taught.
translations.idiomatic: After walking about a li the stranger came back and said, "You can be taught after all.

sentences.zh: 後五日平明，與我期此。」

translations.literal: After five days, at dawn, meet me here."
translations.idiomatic: Five days from now, be here at first light."

sentences.zh: 良因怪之，跪曰：「諾。」

translations.literal: Liang thought it strange, knelt and said, "Yes."
translations.idiomatic: Wondering what it meant, Zhang Liang knelt and answered, "Yes."

sentences.zh: 五日平明，良往。

translations.literal: At dawn on the fifth day Liang went.
translations.idiomatic: At dawn on the fifth day he arrived.

sentences.zh: 父已先在，怒曰：「與老人期，後，何也？

translations.literal: The old man was already there and angrily said, "When you make an appointment with an old man and arrive late, how is that?
translations.idiomatic: The old man was already waiting and snapped, "You kept an elder waiting—how is that proper?

sentences.zh: 去，後五日蚤會。」

translations.literal: Go; after five days meet early."
translations.idiomatic: Come again; this time be here at cockcrow in five days."

sentences.zh: 五日，雞鳴往。

translations.literal: On the fifth day, at cockcrow, he went.
translations.idiomatic: At the crow of the cock on the fifth day he set out.

sentences.zh: 父又先在，復怒曰：「後，何也？

translations.literal: The old man was again there first and again angrily said, "Again late, how is that?
translations.idiomatic: Again the old man was there first and scolded him, "Late again?

sentences.zh: 去，後五日復蚤來。」

translations.literal: Go; after five days come again early."
translations.idiomatic: Go home; in five days arrive earlier still."

sentences.zh: 五日，良夜半往。

translations.literal: On the fifth day Liang went at midnight.
translations.idiomatic: On the fifth night Zhang Liang went at midnight.

sentences.zh: 有頃，父亦來，喜曰：「當如是。」

translations.literal: After a while the old man also came and, pleased, said, "That is how it should be."
translations.idiomatic: Presently the old man appeared, pleased, and said, "That is how it ought to be."

sentences.zh: 出一編書，曰：「讀是則為王者師。

translations.literal: He produced a bundle of writings and said, "Read this and you will be teacher to a king.
translations.idiomatic: He handed him a scroll and said, "Master this and you will counsel a ruler who will found a dynasty.

sentences.zh: 後十年興。

translations.literal: After ten years there will be rise."
translations.idiomatic: In ten years the realm will be in upheaval."

sentences.zh: 十三年，孺子見我，濟北穀城山下黃石即我已。」

translations.literal: In thirteen years the boy will see me; the yellow stone at the foot of Mount Gucheng north of Ji is I."
translations.idiomatic: Thirteen years from now you will find me again as the yellow stone below Mount Gucheng north of the Ji River."

sentences.zh: 遂去不見。

translations.literal: Then he left and was not seen again.
translations.idiomatic: With that he vanished and was never seen again.

sentences.zh: 旦日視其書，乃太公兵法。

translations.literal: The next day Liang looked at the book; it was the Grand Duke's Art of War.
translations.idiomatic: At daybreak Zhang Liang saw that the book was The Grand Duke's Six Secret Teachings.

sentences.zh: 良因異之，常習誦。

translations.literal: Liang thought it extraordinary and often recited and studied it.
translations.idiomatic: He prized it as something rare and pored over it constantly.

sentences.zh: 居下邳，為任俠。

translations.literal: While dwelling at Xiapi he acted as a knight-errant.
translations.idiomatic: At Xiapi he lived the life of a chivalrous adventurer.

sentences.zh: 項伯嘗殺人，從良匿。

translations.literal: Xiang Bo had once killed a man and fled to hide with Liang.
translations.idiomatic: Xiang Bo had committed a killing and took refuge with Zhang Liang.

sentences.zh: 後十年，陳涉等起，良亦聚少年百餘人。

translations.literal: Ten years later Chen She and others rose; Liang also gathered more than a hundred young men.
translations.idiomatic: A decade later, when Chen She and others rebelled, Zhang Liang raised a band of over a hundred youths.

sentences.zh: 景駒自立為楚假王，在留。

translations.literal: Jing Ju declared himself acting King of Chu at Liu.
translations.idiomatic: Jing Ju had proclaimed himself provisional King of Chu at Liu.

sentences.zh: 良欲往從之，行道遇沛公。

translations.literal: Liang wished to go join him; on the road he met the Lord of Pei.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Liang meant to join him but met the Lord of Pei on the way.

sentences.zh: 沛公將數千人略地下邳，遂屬焉。

translations.literal: The Lord of Pei was leading several thousand men to seize territory below Xiapi, and Liang therefore attached himself to him.
translations.idiomatic: The Lord of Pei was leading a few thousand men to secure the area around Xiapi, and Zhang Liang enlisted under him.

sentences.zh: 沛公拜良為廄將。

translations.literal: The Lord of Pei appointed Liang stable commandant.
translations.idiomatic: The Lord of Pei named him stable commandant.

sentences.zh: 良數以太公兵法說沛公，沛公喜，常用其策。

translations.literal: Liang several times expounded the Grand Duke's Art of War to the Lord of Pei; the Lord of Pei was pleased and often used his plans.
translations.idiomatic: Whenever Zhang Liang explained the Grand Duke's military writings, the Lord of Pei grasped them and repeatedly adopted his stratagems.

sentences.zh: 良為它人言，皆不省。

translations.literal: When Liang spoke to others, none understood.
translations.idiomatic: When he tried the same explanations on anyone else, they did not understand.

sentences.zh: 良曰：「沛公殆天授。」

translations.literal: Liang said, "The Lord of Pei is probably heaven-endowed."
translations.idiomatic: He said to himself, "The Lord of Pei must be heaven's chosen instrument."

sentences.zh: 故遂從不去。

translations.literal: Therefore he followed him and did not leave.
translations.idiomatic: So he stayed with him and never left.

sentences.zh: 沛公之薛，見項梁，共立楚懷王。

translations.literal: When the Lord of Pei went to Xue, he saw Xiang Liang, and together they enthroned King Huai of Chu.
translations.idiomatic: When the Lord of Pei went to Xue and met Xiang Liang, they together set up King Huai of Chu.

sentences.zh: 良乃說項梁曰：「君已立楚後，韓諸公子橫陽君成賢，可立為王，益樹黨。」

translations.literal: Liang then urged Xiang Liang, saying, "You have already enthroned a descendant of Chu; among the princes of Han, Hengyangjun Cheng is worthy and can be established as king, to increase your allies."
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Liang then advised Xiang Liang: "You have already placed a scion of Chu on the throne; of Han's princes, Hengyangjun Han Cheng is the ablest—make him king of Han and you widen your coalition."

sentences.zh: 項梁使良求韓成，立為韓王。

translations.literal: Xiang Liang sent Liang to seek Han Cheng and established him as King of Han.
translations.idiomatic: Xiang Liang sent Zhang Liang to fetch Han Cheng and installed him as king of the restored Han.

sentences.zh: 以良為韓司徒，與韓王將千餘人西略韓地，得數城，秦輒復取之，往來為游兵潁川。

translations.literal: He appointed Liang Han's minister of education; with the King of Han he led more than a thousand men west to seize Han territory, gained several cities, but Qin repeatedly retook them, and they went back and forth as roaming troops in Yingchuan.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Liang was made minister of education for Han; with King Han he took a thousand-odd troops west, took several towns only to lose them again to Qin, and skirmished back and forth across Yingchuan as a mobile force.

sentences.zh: 沛公之從雒陽南出轘轅，良引兵從沛公，下韓十餘城，擊楊熊軍。

translations.literal: When the Lord of Pei marched south from Luoyang through Huanyuan, Liang led troops to join the Lord of Pei, took more than ten cities in Han, and struck Yang Xiong's army.
translations.idiomatic: As the Lord of Pei moved south from Luoyang through Huanyuan, Zhang Liang brought his men to his banner, captured a dozen Han cities, and attacked Yang Xiong's host.

sentences.zh: 沛公乃令韓王成留守陽翟，與良俱南，攻下宛，西入武關。

translations.literal: The Lord of Pei then ordered King of Han Cheng to hold Yangzhai while he and Liang went south together, captured Wan, and entered Wuguan from the west.
translations.idiomatic: The Lord of Pei left King Han Cheng to garrison Yangzhai and marched south with Zhang Liang, stormed Wan, and broke into the passes through Wuguan.

sentences.zh: 沛公欲以二萬人擊秦嶢關下軍，良曰：「秦兵尚彊，未可輕。

translations.literal: The Lord of Pei wished to attack the Qin army below the Yao Pass with twenty thousand men. Liang said, "Qin's troops are still strong and cannot be treated lightly.
translations.idiomatic: He was about to hit the Qin force below Yao Pass with twenty thousand men when Zhang Liang warned him: "The Qin army is still formidable—do not underestimate it.

sentences.zh: 臣聞其將屠者子，賈豎易動以利。

translations.literal: I have heard their general is a butcher's son; merchants' sons are easily moved by profit.
translations.idiomatic: Their commander is said to be a butcher's son; men of the market are easily swayed by gain.

sentences.zh: 願沛公且留壁，使人先行，為五萬人具食，益張旗幟諸山上，為疑兵，令酈食其持重寶啗秦將。」

translations.literal: I beg the Lord of Pei to remain in camp for now, send someone ahead to prepare food for fifty thousand men, and widely plant banners on the hills as a decoy force, and have Li Yiji carry heavy treasure to bribe the Qin general."
translations.idiomatic: Stay in your camp awhile, send parties forward to cook for fifty thousand, raise flags on the heights to look like a larger host, and let Li Yiji take rich gifts to buy off the Qin commander."

sentences.zh: 秦將果欲連和俱西襲咸陽，沛公欲聽之。

translations.literal: The Qin general indeed wished to ally and together go west to raid Xianyang; the Lord of Pei wished to consent.
translations.idiomatic: The Qin general offered to join him for a joint strike on Xianyang, and the Lord of Pei was inclined to agree.

sentences.zh: 良曰：「此獨其將欲叛，士卒恐不從。

translations.literal: Liang said, "It is only this general who wishes to rebel; the soldiers probably will not follow.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Liang objected: "Only the general wants to turn traitor—the rank and file may not follow him.

sentences.zh: 不從必危，不如因其解擊之。」

translations.literal: If they do not follow it will surely be perilous; better strike them while they are slack."
translations.idiomatic: If they refuse, you will be in danger; hit them while their guard is down."

sentences.zh: 沛公乃引兵擊秦軍，大破之。

translations.literal: The Lord of Pei then led troops to attack the Qin army and greatly defeated it.
translations.idiomatic: The Lord of Pei attacked and routed the Qin army.

sentences.zh: 逐北至藍田，再戰，秦兵竟敗。

translations.literal: He pursued them north to Lantian, fought again, and the Qin army was finally defeated.
translations.idiomatic: He chased them to Lantian, fought again, and broke them for good.

sentences.zh: 遂至咸陽，秦王子嬰降沛公。

translations.literal: He then reached Xianyang; the Qin prince Ziying surrendered to the Lord of Pei.
translations.idiomatic: He reached Xianyang, where Prince Ziying of Qin surrendered to him.

sentences.zh: 沛公入秦，宮室帷帳狗馬重寶婦女以千數，意欲留居之。

translations.literal: When the Lord of Pei entered Qin, there were palace chambers, curtains, tents, dogs, horses, heavy treasures, and women by the thousands; he intended to remain and dwell there.
translations.idiomatic: Inside the Qin palaces were hangings, hounds, horses, heaps of treasure, and thousands of women; the Lord of Pei meant to move in and stay.

sentences.zh: 樊噲諫，沛公不聽。

translations.literal: Fan Kuai remonstrated but the Lord of Pei would not listen.
translations.idiomatic: Fan Kuai pleaded with him, but he would not listen.

sentences.zh: 良曰：「夫秦為無道，故沛公得至此。

translations.literal: Liang said, "Qin was without the Way; therefore the Lord of Pei was able to reach here.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Liang said, "It was Qin's brutality that brought you this far.

sentences.zh: 為天下除殘去賊，宜縞素為資。

translations.literal: To remove cruelty and banditry for the world, one ought to take plain white garments as one's capital.
translations.idiomatic: You came to rid the empire of a tyrant; your capital should be simplicity and restraint.

sentences.zh: 今始入秦，即安其樂，此所謂『助桀為虐』。

translations.literal: Now having just entered Qin you settle into its pleasures—this is what is called aiding Jie in his cruelty."
translations.idiomatic: To wallow in its luxuries the moment you enter the capital is what people mean by helping Jie torture his people."

sentences.zh: 且『忠言逆耳利於行，毒藥苦口利於病』，願沛公聽樊噲言。」

translations.literal: Moreover, "Loyal words grate on the ear but help conduct; bitter medicine helps the disease"—I beg the Lord of Pei to heed Fan Kuai's words."
translations.idiomatic: Besides, "honest counsel sounds harsh but steadies the deed; bitter medicine heals the illness"—please heed Fan Kuai."

sentences.zh: 沛公乃還軍霸上。

translations.literal: The Lord of Pei then returned his army to Bashang.
translations.idiomatic: The Lord of Pei withdrew his army to Bashang.

sentences.zh: 項羽至鴻門，欲擊沛公，項伯夜馳至沛公軍，私見良，欲與俱去。

translations.literal: When Xiang Yu reached Hongmen he wished to strike the Lord of Pei; Xiang Bo rode by night to the Lord of Pei's army, secretly saw Liang, and wished to go away with him.
translations.idiomatic: At Hongmen Xiang Yu planned to destroy the Lord of Pei; Xiang Bo rode through the night to warn Zhang Liang and tried to take him to safety.

sentences.zh: 良曰：「臣為韓王送沛公，今有事急，亡去不義。」

translations.literal: Liang said, "I am escorting the Lord of Pei for the King of Han; now that matters are urgent, to flee would be unrighteous."
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Liang replied, "I am escorting the Lord of Pei on King Han's orders; to run off now would be dishonorable."

sentences.zh: 乃具語沛公。

translations.literal: He then fully informed the Lord of Pei.
translations.idiomatic: He told the Lord of Pei everything.

sentences.zh: 沛公大驚，曰：「為之柰何？」

translations.literal: The Lord of Pei was greatly alarmed and said, "What is to be done about it?"
translations.idiomatic: The Lord of Pei was terrified and asked what could be done.

sentences.zh: 良曰：「沛公誠欲背項王邪？」

translations.literal: Liang said, "Does the Lord of Pei truly wish to turn against King Xiang?"
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Liang asked, "Do you really mean to break with King Xiang?"

sentences.zh: 沛公曰：「鯫生說我距關毋內諸侯，秦地可王也，故聽之。」

translations.literal: The Lord of Pei said, "A pedant urged me to hold the passes and not admit the lords, so that I could be king in the land of Qin; therefore I listened."
translations.idiomatic: The Lord of Pei said, "A fool told me to bar the passes and keep the other lords out so I could rule Qin myself, and I listened."

sentences.zh: 良曰：「沛公自度能卻項王乎？」

translations.literal: Liang said, "Does the Lord of Pei estimate that he can repel King Xiang?"
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Liang said, "Can you hold him off?"

sentences.zh: 沛公默然，曰：「

translations.literal: The Lord of Pei was silent and said, "
translations.idiomatic: The Lord of Pei fell silent and said, "

sentences.zh: 今為柰何？」

translations.literal: What is to be done now?"
translations.idiomatic: What can I do now?"

sentences.zh: 良因要項伯見沛公。

translations.literal: Liang thereupon invited Xiang Bo to see the Lord of Pei.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Liang then brought Xiang Bo in to meet the Lord of Pei.

sentences.zh: 沛公與伯飲，為壽，結婚，令伯具言沛公不敢背項王，所以距關者，備它盜也。

translations.literal: The Lord of Pei drank with Bo, toasted him, formed a marriage alliance, and ordered Bo to explain in full that the Lord of Pei dared not turn against King Xiang and that the reason for holding the passes was to guard against other bandits.
translations.idiomatic: They drank together, exchanged toasts, pledged their children in marriage, and had Xiang Bo report that the Lord of Pei had no intention of defying Xiang Yu—that he had sealed the passes only against common robbers.

sentences.zh: 項羽後解，語在羽傳。

translations.literal: King Xiang later relented; the account is in Xiang Yu's biography.
translations.idiomatic: Xiang Yu later dropped the matter; the full story is told in his memoir.

sentences.zh: 漢元年，沛公為漢王，王巴蜀，賜良金百溢，珠二斗，良具以獻項伯。

translations.literal: In the first year of Han the Lord of Pei became King of Han, ruling Ba and Shu; he gave Liang a hundred yi of gold and two pecks of pearls, and Liang presented them all to Xiang Bo.
translations.idiomatic: In Han's first year the Lord of Pei became King of Han with Ba and Shu as his domain; he gave Zhang Liang gold by the hundred yi and pearls by the two pecks, and Zhang Liang passed the whole gift to Xiang Bo.

sentences.zh: 漢王亦因令良厚遺項伯，使請漢中地。

translations.literal: The King of Han also thereby ordered Liang to send generous gifts to Xiang Bo and ask for the territory of Hanzhong.
translations.idiomatic: The King of Han also had Zhang Liang send rich presents to Xiang Bo so he might plead for the Hanzhong basin.

sentences.zh: 項王許之。

translations.literal: King Xiang granted it.
translations.idiomatic: Xiang Yu agreed.

sentences.zh: 漢王之國，良送至褒中，遣良歸韓。

translations.literal: When the King of Han went to his kingdom, Liang saw him as far as Baozhong and sent Liang back to Han.
translations.idiomatic: As the King of Han set out for his domain, Zhang Liang accompanied him to Baozhong; the king then sent him back to serve Han Cheng.

sentences.zh: 良因說漢王燒絕棧道，示天下無還心，以固項王意。

translations.literal: Liang therefore urged the King of Han to burn and break the plank roads, showing the world that he had no mind to return, in order to fix King Xiang's mind.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Liang urged him to burn the plank roads through the mountains to signal that he would not march east again, lulling Xiang Yu into complacency.

sentences.zh: 乃使良還。

translations.literal: He then sent Liang back.
translations.idiomatic: The king sent him on his way.

sentences.zh: 行，燒絕棧道。

translations.literal: As he went, he burned and broke the plank roads.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Liang burned the roads as he withdrew.

sentences.zh: 良歸至韓，聞項羽以良從漢王故，不遣韓王成之國，與俱東，至彭城殺之。

translations.literal: When Liang returned to Han, he heard that because Xiang Yu believed Liang had followed the King of Han, he would not let King of Han Cheng go to his kingdom but took him east and killed him at Pengcheng.
translations.idiomatic: Back in Han he learned that Xiang Yu, resenting his service to the King of Han, had refused to let King Han Cheng take up his throne, escorted him east, and executed him at Pengcheng.

sentences.zh: 時漢王還定三秦，良乃遺項羽書曰：「漢王失職，欲得關中，如約即止，不敢復東。」

translations.literal: At that time the King of Han was returning to secure the Three Qin; Liang therefore sent Xiang Yu a letter saying, "The King of Han lost his post and wishes to obtain Guanzhong; according to the agreement he will stop and not dare go east again."
translations.idiomatic: Meanwhile the King of Han was reconquering the Three Qin; Zhang Liang wrote to Xiang Yu that the King of Han only wanted the Guanzhong heartland promised him and would advance no farther east.

sentences.zh: 又以齊反書遺羽，曰：「齊與趙欲并滅楚。」

translations.literal: He also sent Xiang Yu a letter of Qi's rebellion saying, "Qi and Zhao wish together to destroy Chu."
translations.idiomatic: He also forwarded forged letters suggesting that Qi and Zhao were plotting to crush Chu.

sentences.zh: 項羽以故北擊齊。

translations.literal: King Xiang for this reason marched north to attack Qi.
translations.idiomatic: Xiang Yu therefore turned north against Qi.

sentences.zh: 良乃間行歸漢。

translations.literal: Liang then went by secret routes to return to Han.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Liang slipped back to the King of Han by hidden paths.

sentences.zh: 漢王以良為成信侯，從東擊楚。

translations.literal: The King of Han appointed Liang Marquis of Chengxin and followed him east to attack Chu.
translations.idiomatic: The King of Han enfeoffed him as Marquis of Chengxin and took him east against Chu.

sentences.zh: 至彭城，漢王兵敗而還。

translations.literal: When they reached Pengcheng, the King of Han's army was defeated and returned.
translations.idiomatic: At Pengcheng the Han army was shattered and fell back.

sentences.zh: 至下邑，漢王下馬踞鞍而問曰：「吾欲捐關已東等棄之，誰可與共功者？」

translations.literal: When they reached Xiayi, the King of Han dismounted, sat on the saddle, and asked, "I wish to abandon the area east of the passes and give it up; who can share achievement with me?"
translations.idiomatic: At Xiayi the King of Han slid from his saddle, straddled it, and asked, "I am ready to yield everything east of the passes—who can help me win it back?"

sentences.zh: 良曰：「九江王布，楚梟將，與項王有隙，彭越與齊王田榮反梁地，此兩人可急使。

translations.literal: Liang said, "The King of Jiujiang, Bu, is Chu's fiercest general and has a rift with King Xiang; Peng Yue and the King of Qi, Tian Rong, rebelled in Liang territory—these two can be urgently employed.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Liang answered, "Ying Bu of Jiujiang is Chu's most feared commander and bears a grudge against Xiang Yu; Peng Yue and King Qi Tian Rong hold the Liang region in revolt—those two you must enlist at once.

sentences.zh: 而漢王之將獨韓信可屬大事，當一面。

translations.literal: Among the King of Han's generals only Han Xin can be entrusted with great affairs and hold one front.
translations.idiomatic: Of your own captains only Han Xin can shoulder a major front alone.

sentences.zh: 即欲捐之，捐之此三人，楚可破也。」

translations.literal: If you wish to abandon it, abandon it to these three men and Chu can be broken."
translations.idiomatic: If you are willing to give up land, give it to these three and Chu can be destroyed."

sentences.zh: 漢王乃遣隨何說九江王布，而使人連彭越。

translations.literal: The King of Han then sent Sui He to persuade the King of Jiujiang, Bu, and sent men to link up with Peng Yue.
translations.idiomatic: The King of Han dispatched Sui He to win over Ying Bu and sent envoys to coordinate with Peng Yue.

sentences.zh: 及魏王豹反，使韓信特將北擊之，因舉燕、伐、齊、趙。

translations.literal: When King Wei Bao of Wei rebelled, he ordered Han Xin to lead a separate force north to strike him, whereupon he took Yan, Dai, Qi, and Zhao (伐 for the state name is read as 代).
translations.idiomatic: When Wei Bao rebelled, he gave Han Xin an independent northern command; Han Xin went on to overrun Yan, Dai, Qi, and Zhao.

sentences.zh: 然卒破楚者，此三人力也。

translations.literal: Yet in the end those who broke Chu were the strength of these three men.
translations.idiomatic: In the end it was these three who broke Chu's power.

sentences.zh: 良多病，未嘗特將兵，常為畫策臣，時時從。

translations.literal: Liang was often ill and never commanded troops alone; he was always a planning minister and from time to time accompanied him.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Liang was frequently unwell and never held sole field command; he remained a strategist at the king's side, joining campaigns when his health allowed.

sentences.zh: 漢三年，項羽急圍漢王於滎陽，漢王憂恐，與酈食其謀橈楚權。

translations.literal: In the third year of Han, Xiang Yu urgently besieged the King of Han at Yingyang; the King of Han was worried and afraid and consulted Li Yiji on weakening Chu's power.
translations.idiomatic: In Han's third year Xiang Yu had the King of Han tightly bottled up at Yingyang. Beset with fear, the king turned to Li Yiji for a way to sap Chu's strength.

sentences.zh: 酈生曰：「昔湯伐桀，封其後杞；

translations.literal: Li Sheng said, "In antiquity Tang attacked Jie and enfeoffed his descendant at Qi;
translations.idiomatic: Li Yiji said, "When Tang of Shang overthrew Jie, he gave Jie's line a fief at Qi.

sentences.zh: 武王誅紂，封其後宋。

translations.literal: King Wu executed Zhou and enfeoffed his descendant at Song.
translations.idiomatic: When King Wu of Zhou cut down King Zhou of Shang, he let the Shang house survive at Song.

sentences.zh: 今秦無道，伐滅六國，無立錐之地。

translations.literal: Now Qin was without the Way, attacked and extinguished the six states, and left them not ground enough to stick an awl-point.
translations.idiomatic: Qin behaved like a tyrant: it wiped out the six kingdoms and left their royal lines landless.

sentences.zh: 陛下誠復立六國後，此皆爭戴陛下德義，願為臣妾。

translations.literal: If Your Majesty truly restores the descendants of the six states, they will all compete to honor Your Majesty's virtue and righteousness and wish to be your servants and concubines.
translations.idiomatic: If you truly restore those royal houses, their heirs will vie to praise your virtue and gladly call themselves your subjects.

sentences.zh: 德義已行，南面稱伯，楚必斂衽而朝。」

translations.literal: When virtue and righteousness have been practiced, you may face south and declare yourself hegemon; Chu will surely gather its robes and come to court."
translations.idiomatic: Once your righteousness is known, you can face south as sovereign of the realm, and Chu will have to straighten its robes and pay homage."

sentences.zh: 漢王曰：「善。

translations.literal: The King of Han said, "Good.
translations.idiomatic: The King of Han said, "Excellent.

sentences.zh: 趣刻印，先生因行佩之。」

translations.literal: Hurry to carve the seals; you, sir, shall go and carry them as you go."
translations.idiomatic: Have the seals cut at once, Master, and take them on your rounds to bestow."

sentences.zh: 酈生未行，良從外來謁漢王。

translations.literal: Before Li Sheng had set out, Liang came from outside to pay court to the King of Han.
translations.idiomatic: Before Li Yiji could leave, Zhang Liang arrived from outside to see the king.

sentences.zh: 漢王方食，曰：「客有為我計橈楚權者。」

translations.literal: The King of Han was eating and said, "A guest has devised a plan for me to weaken Chu's power."
translations.idiomatic: The king was at table and said, "Someone has brought me a scheme to undercut Xiang Yu."

sentences.zh: 具以酈生計告良曰：「於子房何如？」

translations.literal: He fully told Liang Li Sheng's plan and said, "What of it, Zifang?"
translations.idiomatic: He laid out Li Yiji's proposal and asked, "What do you think, Zifang?"

sentences.zh: 良曰：「誰為陛下畫此計者？

translations.literal: Liang said, "Who drew this plan for Your Majesty?
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Liang asked coldly, "Who sold you this plan?

sentences.zh: 陛下事去矣。」

translations.literal: Your Majesty's cause is lost."
translations.idiomatic: If you follow it, you will lose the empire."

sentences.zh: 漢王曰：「何哉？」

translations.literal: The King of Han said, "How so?"
translations.idiomatic: "How can that be?" the king demanded.

sentences.zh: 良曰：「臣請借前箸以籌之。

translations.literal: Liang said, "Your servant begs to borrow Your Majesty's chopsticks before you to reckon it.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Liang said, "May I use your chopsticks as counters?

sentences.zh: 昔湯武伐桀紂封其後者，度能制其死命也。

translations.literal: In antiquity Tang and Wu attacked Jie and Zhou and enfeoffed their descendants because they could measure their power to hold their life and death in hand.
translations.idiomatic: Tang and Wu could enfeoff the tyrants' heirs only because they already held absolute power over them.

sentences.zh: 今陛下能制項籍死命乎？

translations.literal: Now can Your Majesty control Xiang Ji's life and death?
translations.idiomatic: Can you dictate Xiang Yu's fate the way they could?

sentences.zh: 其不可一矣。

translations.literal: That is the first impossibility.
translations.idiomatic: That is the first reason it will not work.

sentences.zh: 武王入殷，表商容閭，式箕子門，封比干墓，今陛下能乎？

translations.literal: When King Wu entered Yin, he marked Shang Rong's lane, performed the vehicle salute at Jizi's gate, and repaired Bi Gan's tomb; can Your Majesty now?
translations.idiomatic: King Wu honored worthy enemies of Zhou—can you match those gestures today?

sentences.zh: 其不可二矣。

translations.literal: That is the second impossibility.
translations.idiomatic: Second impossibility.

sentences.zh: 發鉅橋之粟，散鹿臺之財，以賜貧窮，今陛下能乎？

translations.literal: He released the grain at Juliao, scattered the wealth of Lutai, and gave it to the poor; can Your Majesty now?
translations.idiomatic: He opened the granaries and gave the hoarded wealth to the people—can you afford that now?

sentences.zh: 其不可三矣。

translations.literal: That is the third impossibility.
translations.idiomatic: Third impossibility.

sentences.zh: 殷事以畢，偃革為軒，倒載干戈，示不復用，今陛下能乎？

translations.literal: When the Yin affair was finished, he laid aside shields for carriages, inverted spears and halberds to show they would not be used again; can Your Majesty now?
translations.idiomatic: When the war ended he melted the weapons into ploughshares—can you declare peace finished?

sentences.zh: 其不可四矣。

translations.literal: That is the fourth impossibility.
translations.idiomatic: Fourth impossibility.

sentences.zh: 休馬華山之陽，示無所為，今陛下能乎？

translations.literal: He rested horses on the south slope of Mount Hua to show there was nothing more to do; can Your Majesty now?
translations.idiomatic: He pastured the warhorses on Mount Hua's sunny slopes to show the world the sword was sheathed—can you?

sentences.zh: 其不可五矣。

translations.literal: That is the fifth impossibility.
translations.idiomatic: Fifth impossibility.

sentences.zh: 息牛桃林之野，示天下不復輸積，今陛下能乎？

translations.literal: He rested oxen in the wilds of Taolin to show the empire would not again transport stores; can Your Majesty now?
translations.idiomatic: He turned the draft oxen loose in Taolin to signal that no more levies of grain would march to war—can you?

sentences.zh: 其不可六矣。

translations.literal: That is the sixth impossibility.
translations.idiomatic: Sixth impossibility.

sentences.zh: 且夫天下游士，左親戚，棄墳墓，去故舊，從陛下者，但日夜望咫尺之地。

translations.literal: Moreover the wandering knights of the world left kin, abandoned tombs, left old friends, and followed Your Majesty only because day and night they hoped for a handspan of territory.
translations.idiomatic: Your army of adventurers gave up home and family to follow you for one reason: they expect a slice of land when the fighting stops.

sentences.zh: 今乃立六國後，唯無復立者，游士各歸事其主，從親戚，反故舊，陛下誰與取天下乎？

translations.literal: Now if you establish the heirs of the six states, as for those alone without a restoration to offer, wandering knights will each return to serve their masters, follow kin, revert to old friends—with whom will Your Majesty take the world?
translations.idiomatic: Restore six old royal lines, and every strategist still at your side will drift back to lords who can actually reward them—who then helps you win the realm?

sentences.zh: 其不可七矣。

translations.literal: That is the seventh impossibility.
translations.idiomatic: Seventh impossibility.

sentences.zh: 且楚唯毋彊，六國復橈而從之，陛下焉得而臣之？

translations.literal: Moreover if Chu alone is not weakened and the six states, restored, bend to follow it, how will Your Majesty obtain them as subjects?
translations.idiomatic: Worse, if Chu stays strong while those new kings look to Xiang Yu for protection, how will you ever bring them to heel?

sentences.zh: 其不可八矣。

translations.literal: That is the eighth impossibility.
translations.idiomatic: Eighth impossibility.

sentences.zh: 誠用此謀，陛下事去矣。」

translations.literal: If this plan is truly used, Your Majesty's cause is lost."
translations.idiomatic: Adopt this counsel and your cause is finished."

sentences.zh: 漢王輟食吐哺，罵曰：「豎儒，幾敗乃公事！」

translations.literal: The King of Han stopped eating, spat out his food, and cursed, "That pedant nearly ruined my great affair!"
translations.idiomatic: The king pushed his meal aside, spat out his mouthful, and swore, "That blasted pedant nearly wrecked everything I have built!"

sentences.zh: 令趣銷印。

translations.literal: He ordered the seals melted down at once.
translations.idiomatic: He ordered the new seals destroyed immediately.

sentences.zh: 後韓信破齊欲自立為齊王，漢王怒。

translations.literal: Later Han Xin broke Qi and wished to declare himself King of Qi; the King of Han was angry.
translations.idiomatic: Later Han Xin crushed Qi and demanded to be named its king; the King of Han was furious.

sentences.zh: 良說漢王，漢王使良授齊王信印。

translations.literal: Liang persuaded the King of Han; the King of Han sent Liang to present the seal of King of Qi to Xin.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Liang talked him round, and the king sent Zhang Liang to invest Han Xin as king of Qi.

sentences.zh: 語在信傳。

translations.literal: The account is in Xin's biography.
translations.idiomatic: The details are recorded in Han Xin's memoir.

sentences.zh: 五年冬，漢王追楚至陽夏南，戰不利，壁固陵，諸侯期不至。

translations.literal: In the fifth year, winter, the King of Han pursued Chu south of Yangxia; the battle went badly; he fortified Guling; the lords did not arrive at the appointed time.
translations.idiomatic: In the fifth year, winter, the King of Han chased Chu to the south of Yangxia, lost the engagement, dug in at Guling, and waited in vain for his allies.

sentences.zh: 良說漢王，漢王用其計，諸侯皆至。

translations.literal: Liang persuaded the King of Han; the King of Han used his plan and the lords all arrived.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Liang proposed a stratagem; the king adopted it and the coalition forces appeared.

sentences.zh: 語在高紀。

translations.literal: The account is in the Basic Annals of Gao.
translations.idiomatic: The full story is told in Emperor Gao's annals.

sentences.zh: 漢六年，封功臣。

translations.literal: In the sixth year of Han he enfeoffed the merit ministers.
translations.idiomatic: In Han's sixth year the throne began enfeoffing those who had earned it.

sentences.zh: 良未嘗有戰鬥功，高帝曰：「運籌策帷幄中，決勝千里外，子房功也。

translations.literal: Liang had never had battle merit; the August Emperor said, "To plan strategies within the tent and decide victory a thousand li away—that is Zifang's merit.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Liang had never led troops in the field. The High Emperor said, "Victory was decided in the command tent a thousand li from the battlefield—that was Zifang's work.

sentences.zh: 自擇齊三萬戶。」

translations.literal: Choose for yourself thirty thousand households in Qi."
translations.idiomatic: Take any thirty thousand households you wish in Qi."

sentences.zh: 良曰：「始臣起下邳，與上會留，此天以臣授陛下。

translations.literal: Liang said, "When your servant first rose at Xiapi and met Your Majesty at Liu, heaven thereby gave your servant to Your Majesty.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Liang said, "When I first joined you at Liu, heaven was handing me to you as a tool.

sentences.zh: 陛下用臣計，幸而時中，臣願封留足矣，不敢當三萬戶。」

translations.literal: Your Majesty used your servant's plans, fortunate that they sometimes hit the mark; your servant wishes to be enfeoffed at Liu alone and does not dare accept thirty thousand households."
translations.idiomatic: Your strategies sometimes chanced to succeed; I ask only for the marquisate of Liu—I could not presume on thirty thousand households."

sentences.zh: 乃封良為留侯，與蕭何等俱封。

translations.literal: Therefore he enfeoffed Liang as Marquis of Liu, together with Xiao He and others being enfeoffed.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor made him Marquis of Liu alongside Xiao He and the rest.

sentences.zh: 上已封大功臣二十餘人，其餘日夜爭功而不決，未得行封。

translations.literal: The emperor had already enfeoffed more than twenty great merit ministers; the rest disputed merit day and night without resolution, and enfeoffments could not proceed.
translations.idiomatic: More than twenty chief generals were already titled, but the remainder wrangled over precedence day and night, so no further patents could be issued.

sentences.zh: 上居雒陽南宮，從復道望見諸將往往數人偶語。

translations.literal: The emperor dwelt in the Southern Palace at Luoyang; from the covered walk he often saw several of the generals whispering in pairs.
translations.idiomatic: From a gallery of the Southern Palace at Luoyang he watched clusters of officers muttering in corners.

sentences.zh: 上曰：「此何語？」

translations.literal: The emperor said, "What are they saying?"
translations.idiomatic: He asked what they were plotting.

sentences.zh: 良曰：「陛下不知乎？

translations.literal: Liang said, "Does Your Majesty not know?
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Liang said, "Can you not guess?

sentences.zh: 此謀反耳。」

translations.literal: This is plotting rebellion."
translations.idiomatic: They are talking treason."

sentences.zh: 上曰：「天下屬安定，何故而反？」

translations.literal: The emperor said, "The realm is settling under me; why would they rebel?"
translations.idiomatic: The emperor protested, "The empire is at peace—why should they turn traitor?"

sentences.zh: 良曰：「陛下起布衣，與此屬取天下，今陛下已為天子，而所封皆蕭、曹故人所親愛，而所誅者皆平生仇怨。

translations.literal: Liang said, "Your Majesty rose from common cloth and with this group seized the world; now Your Majesty is Son of Heaven, but those enfeoffed are all Xiao's and Cao's old friends whom you love, while those executed are all lifelong enemies and resentments.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Liang answered, "You began as a commoner and won the realm with these men. Now you are emperor, yet the new nobles are your old cronies from Pei while everyone you execute is someone you have long hated.

sentences.zh: 今軍吏計功，天下不足以遍封，此屬畏陛下不能盡封，又恐見疑過失及誅，故相聚而謀反耳。」

translations.literal: Now the officers reckon merit; the world is not enough to enfeoff them all; this group fears Your Majesty cannot enfeoff them all and also fears being suspected of faults and executed; therefore they gather and plot rebellion."
translations.idiomatic: They keep tally of their deeds, and there is not enough land in China to pay them all. They are afraid you will not reward everyone and even more afraid a slip will cost their heads—so they huddle and scheme."

sentences.zh: 上乃憂曰：「為將柰何？」

translations.literal: The emperor then worried and said, "What is to be done about the generals?"
translations.idiomatic: The emperor said anxiously, "What should I do about them?"

sentences.zh: 良曰：「上平生所憎，群臣所共知，誰最甚者？」

translations.literal: Liang said, "Among those Your Majesty has hated all his life, whom the assembled ministers all know, who is the worst?"
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Liang asked, "Of all the men you personally detest, whom do your officers know you despise most?"

sentences.zh: 上曰：「雍齒與我有故怨，數窘辱我，我欲殺之，為功多，不忍。」

translations.literal: The emperor said, "Yong Chi has old resentment with me, repeatedly embarrassed and insulted me; I wished to kill him, but because his merit was great I forbore."
translations.idiomatic: He answered, "Yong Chi and I go way back as enemies; he has humiliated me again and again. I have long wanted him dead, yet his service was too great to ignore."

sentences.zh: 良曰：「今急先封雍齒，以示群臣，群臣見雍齒先封，則人人自堅矣。」

translations.literal: Liang said, "Now urgently first enfeoff Yong Chi to show the assembled ministers; when the assembled ministers see Yong Chi first enfeoffed, then each person will himself be firm."
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Liang said, "Give Yong Chi a patent first, and publicly. Once they see even Yong Chi ennobled, every man will feel secure."

sentences.zh: 於是上置酒，封雍齒為什方侯，而急趣丞相御史定功行封。

translations.literal: Thereupon the emperor set out wine, enfeoffed Yong Chi as Marquis of Shifang, and urgently urged the chancellor and imperial secretary to fix merit and carry out enfeoffments.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor held a banquet, named Yong Chi marquis of Shifang, and told his ministers to hurry the rolls of merit.

sentences.zh: 群臣罷酒，皆喜曰：「雍齒且侯，我屬無患矣。」

translations.literal: When the assembled ministers finished the wine, they all joyfully said, "Yong Chi is even a marquis; we have no worry."
translations.idiomatic: When the drinking ended the captains said to one another, "If Yong Chi gets a fief, the rest of us are safe."

sentences.zh: 劉敬說上都關中，上疑之。

translations.literal: Liu Jing urged the emperor to make his capital in Guanzhong; the emperor hesitated.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Jing urged him to move the capital into the passes; the emperor was unsure.

sentences.zh: 左右大臣皆山東人，多勸上都雒陽：「雒陽東有成皋，西有殽黽，背河鄉雒，其固亦足恃。」

translations.literal: The ministers at his left and right were all men east of the mountains and mostly urged the emperor to make his capital at Luoyang: "Luoyang has east of it Chenggao, west of it Yao and Min, with its back to the river facing Luo; its strength is also enough to rely on."
translations.idiomatic: Most of his council were easterners who argued for Luoyang: east lies Chenggao, west the Yao and Min defiles, with the Yellow River at your back and the Luo before you—surely that is strong enough."

sentences.zh: 良曰：「雒陽雖有此固，其中小，不過數百里，田地薄，四面受敵，此非用武之國。

translations.literal: Liang said, "Although Luoyang has these strengths, its interior is small, not exceeding several hundred li, the fields are thin, and it receives enemies on four sides; this is not a state for wielding military power.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Liang replied, "Luoyang has those barriers, yet its core is cramped, its farmland poor, and it is exposed on every side—not a seat for a warlike dynasty.

sentences.zh: 夫關中左殽函，右隴蜀，沃野千里，南有巴蜀之饒，北有胡苑之利，阻三面而固守，獨以一面東制諸侯。

translations.literal: Guanzhong has left Yao and Han, right Long and Shu, a thousand li of fertile wilds, south the abundance of Ba and Shu, north the profit of the Hu pastures; it blocks three sides and holds firm, using only one side eastward to control the lords.
translations.idiomatic: The Guanzhong basin is boxed by Yao and Han on the left, Long and Shu on the right, with a thousand li of rich soil, the wealth of Ba and Shu to the south, and the horse country of the steppe to the north. Three sides are shielded by nature; you need only face east toward your vassals.

sentences.zh: 諸侯安定，河、渭漕輓天下，西給京師；

translations.literal: When the lords are settled, the Yellow River and Wei can transport grain to supply the world and feed the western capital westward;
translations.idiomatic: In peace the Wei and Yellow River canals can float tribute to the capital;

sentences.zh: 諸侯有變，順流而下，足以委輸。

translations.literal: if the lords change, going downstream with the current is enough to convey supplies.
translations.idiomatic: in revolt you can float supplies downriver against them.

sentences.zh: 此所謂金城千里，天府之國。

translations.literal: This is what is called a metal city a thousand li and a heaven-sent treasury state.
translations.idiomatic: That is the true "city of bronze walls" and "treasury heaven built."

sentences.zh: 劉敬說是也。」

translations.literal: Liu Jing's persuasion is right."
translations.idiomatic: Liu Jing is right."

sentences.zh: 於是上即日駕，西都關中。

translations.literal: Thereupon the emperor that same day set out his carriage and made his western capital in Guanzhong.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor struck camp the same day and moved the seat of power west into the passes.

sentences.zh: 良從入關，性多疾，即道引不食穀，閉門不出歲餘。

translations.literal: Liang followed in entering the passes; his nature had many illnesses; he then practiced daoyin and did not eat grain, closed his door and did not go out for more than a year.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Liang followed him in but was chronically ill; he took up breathing exercises, gave up grain, and kept to his house for over a year.

sentences.zh: 上欲廢太子，立戚夫人子趙王如意。

translations.literal: The emperor wished to depose the heir apparent and establish Lady Qi's son, King Zhao Ruyi.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor wanted to replace the crown prince with the son of Lady Qi, Prince Ruyi of Zhao.

sentences.zh: 大臣多爭，未能得堅決也。

translations.literal: The great ministers mostly disputed it and could not obtain a firm decision.
translations.idiomatic: His ministers argued fiercely, yet he would not drop the idea.

sentences.zh: 呂后恐，不知所為。

translations.literal: Empress Lü was afraid and did not know what to do.
translations.idiomatic: Empress Lü was frantic and saw no way out.

sentences.zh: 或謂呂后曰：「留侯善畫計，上信用之。」

translations.literal: Someone said to Empress Lü, "The Marquis of Liu is skilled at drawing plans, and the emperor trusts and employs him."
translations.idiomatic: Someone told her, "Zhang Liang still plots the subtlest schemes, and the emperor listens to him."

sentences.zh: 呂后乃使建成侯呂澤劫良，曰：「君常為上謀臣，今上日欲易太子，君安得高枕而臥？」

translations.literal: Empress Lü therefore had Marquis of Jiancheng Lü Ze seize Liang and say, "You have always been the emperor's planning minister; now the emperor daily wishes to change the heir apparent—how can you sleep on a high pillow?"
translations.idiomatic: She sent her brother Lü Ze to corner Zhang Liang and demand, "You have always been his strategist; now he means to swap the heir every day—how can you stay in bed?"

sentences.zh: 良曰：「始上數在急困之中，幸用臣策；

translations.literal: Liang said, "In the beginning the emperor was repeatedly in urgent distress and fortunately used your servant's strategies;
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Liang answered, "In the old days of danger he needed my counsel.

sentences.zh: 今天下安定，以愛欲易太子，骨肉之間，雖臣等百人何益！」

translations.literal: Now the world is settled; to change the heir apparent through love and desire—among flesh and blood, even if we ministers were a hundred men, what good?"
translations.idiomatic: Now the realm is quiet, yet he would tear up the succession for a whim of the heart. That is a family quarrel; a hundred ministers like me cannot sway it."

sentences.zh: 呂澤彊要曰：「為我畫計。」

translations.literal: Lü Ze forcibly demanded, "Draw a plan for me."
translations.idiomatic: Lü Ze would not let him go: "You must give us a plan."

sentences.zh: 良曰：「此難以口舌爭也。

translations.literal: Liang said, "This is difficult to dispute with mouth and tongue.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Liang said, "This cannot be argued in words.

sentences.zh: 顧上有所不能致者四人。

translations.literal: But there are four men whom the emperor cannot summon.
translations.idiomatic: There are four elders whom the emperor has tried in vain to court.

sentences.zh: 四人年老矣，皆以上嫚厉士，故逃匿山中，義不為漢臣。

translations.literal: The four men are old; all because the emperor insults and mistreats scholars, therefore they fled and hid in the mountains and by righteousness will not be Han ministers.
translations.idiomatic: They are aged recluses who fled to the hills because they despised his rough treatment of scholars and refuse to serve Han.

sentences.zh: 然上高此四人。

translations.literal: Yet the emperor esteems these four men.
translations.idiomatic: Yet nothing would please him more than to have them at court.

sentences.zh: 今公誠能毋愛金玉璧帛，令太子為書，卑辭安車，因使辨士固請，宜來。

translations.literal: Now if you, sir, can truly not begrudge gold, jade, disks, and silks, order the heir apparent to write a letter, with humble words and a comfortable carriage, and thereby send a persuasive man earnestly to invite them—they ought to come.
translations.idiomatic: Spend freely from the privy purse: have the crown prince send a letter of deepest humility, dispatch a cushioned carriage, and send an eloquent envoy to beg them—they will come.

sentences.zh: 來，以為客，時從入朝，令上見之，則一助也。」

translations.literal: When they come, treat them as guests; from time to time follow into court and let the emperor see them; then it is one help."
translations.idiomatic: Install them as the prince's tutors and march them into an audience—one glimpse may be enough to give the emperor pause."

sentences.zh: 於是呂后令呂澤使人奉太子書，卑辭厚禮，迎此四人。

translations.literal: Thereupon Empress Lü ordered Lü Ze to send men carrying the heir apparent's letter, with humble words and generous gifts, to welcome these four men.
translations.idiomatic: Empress Lü had Lü Ze send messengers with the prince's letter, rich gifts, and humble pleas.

sentences.zh: 四人至，客建成侯所。

translations.literal: The four men arrived and were guests at the Marquis of Jiancheng's residence.
translations.idiomatic: The four elders arrived and lodged with Lü Ze.

sentences.zh: 漢十二年，上從破布歸，疾益甚，愈欲易太子。

translations.literal: In the twelfth year of Han the emperor returned from defeating Bu; his illness grew worse and he increasingly wished to change the heir apparent.
translations.idiomatic: In Han's twelfth year, returning from the campaign against Ying Bu, the emperor fell gravely ill and only pressed harder to replace the heir.

sentences.zh: 良諫不聽，因疾不視事。

translations.literal: Liang remonstrated but was not listened to; therefore on account of illness he did not attend to affairs.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Liang protested in vain, then pleaded illness and withdrew from government.

sentences.zh: 叔孫太傅稱說引古，以死爭太子。

translations.literal: Grand Tutor Sunshu cited antiquity and argued, staking his life on disputing for the heir apparent.
translations.idiomatic: Grand Tutor Sunshu Tong lectured him with classical precedents and offered his life to save the succession.

sentences.zh: 上陽許之，猶欲易之。

translations.literal: The emperor falsely promised him but still wished to change him.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor pretended to agree yet meant to go through with the change.

sentences.zh: 及宴，置酒，太子侍。

translations.literal: When it came to a banquet where wine was set out, the heir apparent attended.
translations.idiomatic: At a palace banquet the crown prince stood attendance.

sentences.zh: 四人者從太子，年皆八十有餘，須眉皓白，衣冠甚偉。

translations.literal: The four men followed the heir apparent; their years were all more than eighty; their beards and brows were hoary white; their caps and robes were very grand.
translations.idiomatic: Four white-haired men over eighty walked in his train, their hair and brows snow-white, their dress imposing.

sentences.zh: 上怪，問曰：「何為者？」

translations.literal: The emperor wondered and asked, "What are they?"
translations.idiomatic: The emperor stared and asked who they were.

sentences.zh: 四人前對，各言其姓名。

translations.literal: The four men stepped forward and answered, each stating his name.
translations.idiomatic: They stepped forward and gave their names.

sentences.zh: 上乃驚曰：「吾求公，避逃我，今公何自從吾兒游乎？」

translations.literal: The emperor was startled and said, "I sought you gentlemen; you fled and hid from me—why do you now follow my son on his travels?"
translations.idiomatic: The emperor exclaimed, "I searched everywhere for you; you hid from me—why do you walk with my son?"

sentences.zh: 四人曰：「陛下輕士善罵，臣等義不辱，故恐而亡匿。

translations.literal: The four men said, "Your Majesty slighted scholars and was good at cursing; we, your servants, by righteousness would not suffer insult, therefore we feared and fled into hiding.
translations.idiomatic: They answered, "You scorned men of learning and loved to abuse them; we would not endure the humiliation and fled.

sentences.zh: 今聞太子仁孝，恭敬愛士，天下莫不延頸願為太子死者，故臣等來。」

translations.literal: Now we have heard the heir apparent is benevolent and filial, respectful and loving to scholars; there is none in the world who does not crane his neck wishing to die for the heir apparent; therefore we, your servants, have come."
translations.idiomatic: We hear the crown prince is humane, dutiful, and reverent toward scholars; the whole realm strains to die for him—so we came."

sentences.zh: 上曰：「煩公幸卒調護太子。」

translations.literal: The emperor said, "I trouble you gentlemen; please in the end regulate and protect the heir apparent."
translations.idiomatic: The emperor said, "Then I beg you to watch over him for me."

sentences.zh: 四人為壽已畢，趨去。

translations.literal: The four men finished their toast for longevity and hurried away.
translations.idiomatic: When the toasts were done, the four elders withdrew in haste.

sentences.zh: 上目送之，召戚夫人指視曰：「我欲易之，彼四人為之輔，羽翼已成，難動矣。

translations.literal: The emperor watched them with his eyes until they were gone, summoned Lady Qi, pointed and showed her, saying, "I wished to replace him, but those four men are his helpers; the wings are already formed—difficult to move.
translations.idiomatic: He watched them go, then called Lady Qi over and said, "I meant to change the heir, but those four old men stand behind him now; his party is full-grown—I cannot touch him.

sentences.zh: 呂氏真乃主矣。」

translations.literal: The house of Lü will truly be master."
translations.idiomatic: The Lü family will rule this house after all."

sentences.zh: 戚夫人泣涕，上曰：「為我楚舞，吾為若楚歌。」

translations.literal: Lady Qi wept; the emperor said, "Perform the Chu dance for me; I will sing a Chu song for you."
translations.idiomatic: Lady Qi sobbed; he told her to dance the old Chu measures while he sang for her.

sentences.zh: 歌曰：「鴻鵠高飛，一舉千里。

translations.literal: The song said, "The swan goose flies high, one stroke a thousand li.
translations.idiomatic: His song ran, "The wild swan beats upward—one stroke, a thousand li.

sentences.zh: 羽翼以就，橫絕四海。

translations.literal: Its wings are already formed, spanning the four seas.
translations.idiomatic: Its pinions are set; it rides the wind across the seas.

sentences.zh: 橫絕四海，又可奈何！

translations.literal: Spanning the four seas—what can be done!
translations.idiomatic: It spans the four seas—what can you do?

sentences.zh: 雖有矰繳，尚安所施！」

translations.literal: Though there are stringed arrows and bolts, where can they be applied!"
translations.idiomatic: Though you string the zeng bolts, what target is left to strike!"

sentences.zh: 歌數闋，戚夫人歔欷流涕，上起去，罷酒。

translations.literal: After several stanzas of the song, Lady Qi sobbed and wept; the emperor rose and left, ending the wine.
translations.idiomatic: He sang verse after verse; Lady Qi wept into her sleeves. The emperor rose, cut short the banquet, and left.

sentences.zh: 竟不易太子者，良本招此四人之力也。

translations.literal: That in the end he did not change the heir apparent was fundamentally due to the power of these four men whom Liang had summoned.
translations.idiomatic: The crown prince was never deposed because Zhang Liang had brought those four men into play.

sentences.zh: 良始所見下邳圯上老父與書者，後十三歲從高帝過濟北，果得穀城山下黃石，取而寶祠之。

translations.literal: The old man on the bridge at Xiapi whom Liang first saw and who gave him a book—thirteen years later, following the High Emperor past the north of Ji, he indeed obtained the yellow stone below Mount Gucheng, took it, and treasured and sacrificed to it.
translations.idiomatic: The greybeard who met him on the Xiapi bridge and gave him a book—thirteen years later, marching with the High Emperor through northern Ji, Zhang Liang found the yellow stone beneath Mount Gucheng as foretold, took it home, and honored it like a tutelary god.

sentences.zh: 及良死，并葬黃石。

translations.literal: When Liang died, he was buried together with the yellow stone.
translations.idiomatic: When he died, that stone was laid in his tomb with him.

sentences.zh: 每上冢伏臘祠黃石。

translations.literal: Each time they ascended the tomb at the winter and summer sacrifices they sacrificed to the yellow stone.
translations.idiomatic: Every New Year and midsummer offering at his grave, the family sacrificed to the stone as well.

sentences.zh: 子不疑嗣侯。

translations.literal: His son Buyi inherited the marquisate.
translations.idiomatic: His son Zhang Buyi succeeded to the title.

sentences.zh: 孝文三年坐不敬，國除。

translations.literal: In the third year of Emperor Xiaowen he sat guilty of irreverence; the state was abolished.
translations.idiomatic: In the third year of Emperor Wen he lost the fief for an offense of disrespect.

sentences.zh: 陳平，陽武戶牖鄉人也。

translations.literal: Chen Ping was a man of Huyou Township in Yangwu.
translations.idiomatic: Chen Ping came from Huyou township in Yangwu county.

sentences.zh: 少時家貧，好讀書，治黃帝、老子之術。

translations.literal: In youth his family was poor; he loved to read books and cultivated the arts of the Yellow Emperor and Laozi.
translations.idiomatic: He grew up in want but loved books and steeped himself in the teachings of the Yellow Emperor and Laozi.

sentences.zh: 有田三十畝，與兄伯居。

translations.literal: He had thirty mu of fields and dwelt with his elder brother Bo.
translations.idiomatic: He held thirty mu of land and lived with his older brother Bo.

sentences.zh: 伯常耕田，縱平使游學。

translations.literal: Bo always plowed the fields and let Ping roam to study.
translations.idiomatic: Bo worked the soil himself and freed Chen Ping to travel and study.

sentences.zh: 平為人長大美色，人或謂平：「貧何食而肥若是？」

translations.literal: Ping as a man was tall and handsome in appearance; someone said to Ping, "Being poor, what do you eat to be fat like this?"
translations.idiomatic: Chen Ping was tall and strikingly handsome; people asked how a poor man stayed so plump.

sentences.zh: 其嫂疾平之不親家生產，曰：「亦食糠覈耳。

translations.literal: His sister-in-law hated Ping for not personally attending to household production and said, "He eats only husks and chaff.
translations.idiomatic: His sister-in-law resented his refusal to work the land and sneered, "He lives on bran and husks alone.

sentences.zh: 有叔如此，不如無有！」

translations.literal: To have an uncle like this is not as good as having none!"
translations.idiomatic: An uncle like that is worse than none at all!"

sentences.zh: 伯聞之，逐其婦棄之。

translations.literal: When Bo heard it, he drove away his wife and cast her off.
translations.idiomatic: Bo heard her, cast her out, and divorced her.

sentences.zh: 及平長，可取婦，富人莫與者，貧者平亦媿之。

translations.literal: When Ping grew to the age to take a wife, the rich would not give him one, and Ping was ashamed to take a poor one.
translations.idiomatic: When it was time to marry, rich families would have none of him, and he scorned to wed down into poverty.

sentences.zh: 久之，戶牖富人張負有女孫，五嫁夫輒死，人莫敢取，平欲得之。

translations.literal: After a long time, there was a rich man of Huyou, Zhang Fu, who had a granddaughter; five times she married and the husband always died; no one dared take her; Ping wished to obtain her.
translations.idiomatic: Eventually Zhang Fu, a wealthy neighbor, had a granddaughter whose five husbands had all died young; no one would wed her, and Chen Ping set his heart on the match.

sentences.zh: 邑中有大喪，平家貧侍喪，以先往後罷為助。

translations.literal: There was a great funeral in the town; Ping's family was poor and attended the funeral, taking as help arriving first and leaving last.
translations.idiomatic: A great house in the district held a funeral; though poor, Chen Ping served as a hired mourner, always first to arrive and last to leave.

sentences.zh: 張負既見之喪所，獨視偉平，平亦以故後去。

translations.literal: Zhang Fu, having seen him at the funeral place, alone regarded Ping as remarkable; Ping also for this reason left late.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Fu noticed him at the wake and marked him as extraordinary; Chen Ping lingered on purpose.

sentences.zh: 負隨平至其家，家乃負郭窮巷，以席為門，然門外多長者車轍。

translations.literal: Fu followed Ping to his home; the home was against the outer wall in a poor lane, using a mat for a door, yet outside the door were many tracks of elders' carriages.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Fu followed him home—a hovel outside the city wall with a reed mat for a door—yet the lane was scored with the wheels of visiting notables.

sentences.zh: 張負歸，謂其子仲曰：「吾欲以女孫予陳平。」

translations.literal: Zhang Fu returned and said to his son Zhong, "I wish to give my granddaughter to Chen Ping."
translations.idiomatic: He told his son Zhong, "I mean to give my granddaughter to Chen Ping."

sentences.zh: 仲曰：「平貧不事事，一縣中盡笑其所為，獨柰何予之女？」

translations.literal: Zhong said, "Ping is poor and does not attend to affairs; the whole county laughs at what he does—how alone give him your daughter?"
translations.idiomatic: Zhong objected, "The whole town mocks that idler—why give him our girl?"

sentences.zh: 負曰：「固有美如陳平長貧者乎？」

translations.literal: Fu said, "Is there indeed one as handsome as Chen Ping who remains long poor?"
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Fu answered, "Can a man of such bearing stay poor forever?"

sentences.zh: 卒與女。

translations.literal: In the end he gave him the daughter.
translations.idiomatic: In the end he gave him the bride.

sentences.zh: 為平貧，乃假貸幣以聘，予酒肉之資以內婦。

translations.literal: Because Ping was poor, he borrowed money for the betrothal gifts and gave resources of wine and meat to bring in the wife.
translations.idiomatic: He lent him bride-price money and paid for the wedding feast himself.

sentences.zh: 負戒其孫曰：「毋以貧故，事人不謹。

translations.literal: Fu admonished his granddaughter, saying, "Do not because of poverty serve your husband without care.
translations.idiomatic: He warned the bride, "Do not slack in duty because your husband is poor.

sentences.zh: 事兄伯如事乃父，事嫂如事乃母。」

translations.literal: Serve elder brother Bo as you serve your father; serve your sister-in-law as you serve your mother."
translations.idiomatic: Honor Bo as you would a father and his wife as you would a mother."

sentences.zh: 平既取張氏女，資用益饒，游道日廣。

translations.literal: After Ping had taken the daughter of the Zhang clan, his resources and supplies became more abundant and his path of roaming daily wider.
translations.idiomatic: Once married into the Zhangs, Chen Ping had money to spare and his circle widened.

sentences.zh: 里中社，平為宰，分肉甚均。

translations.literal: At the village earth-god sacrifice, Ping was steward and divided the meat very evenly.
translations.idiomatic: When the hamlet sacrificed to the soil god, Chen Ping carved the sacrificial meat and portioned it with perfect fairness.

sentences.zh: 里父老曰：「善，陳孺子之為宰！」

translations.literal: The village elders said, "Good—the young Chen as steward!"
translations.idiomatic: The elders praised him: "Well done, young Chen, as master of the feast!"

sentences.zh: 平曰：「嗟乎，使平得宰天下，亦如此肉矣！」

translations.literal: Ping said, "Alas, if Ping could be steward of the world, it would also be like this meat!"
translations.idiomatic: Chen Ping laughed and said, "If I ever carved up the empire, I would wield the knife just as evenly!"

sentences.zh: 陳涉起王，使周市略地，立魏咎為魏王，與秦軍相攻於臨濟。

translations.literal: Chen She rose and declared himself king; he sent Zhou Shi to seize territory, established Wei Jiu as King of Wei, and fought Qin's army at Linji.
translations.idiomatic: When Chen She declared himself king, Zhou Shi campaigned in the east, set Wei Jiu on the throne of Wei, and met the Qin army at Linji.

sentences.zh: 平已前謝兄伯，從少年往事魏王咎，為太僕。

translations.literal: Ping had earlier taken leave of elder brother Bo and followed young men to serve King Wei Jiu as grand coachman.
translations.idiomatic: Chen Ping had said farewell to Bo and joined other youths in Wei Jiu's service as grand coachman.

sentences.zh: 說魏王，王不聽。

translations.literal: He remonstrated with the King of Wei; the king would not listen.
translations.idiomatic: He offered counsel; the king ignored it.

sentences.zh: 人或讒之，平亡去。

translations.literal: Someone slandered him; Ping fled and left.
translations.idiomatic: Slander reached the king, and Chen Ping slipped away.

sentences.zh: 項羽略地至河上，平往歸之，從入破秦，賜爵卿。

translations.literal: When Xiang Yu seized territory as far as the Yellow River, Ping went to join him, followed in entering and breaking Qin, and was granted the noble rank of qing.
translations.idiomatic: When Xiang Yu's host reached the Yellow River, Chen Ping entered his camp, followed him into the Guanzhong heartland, and was ennobled as a "minister" rank lord.

sentences.zh: 項羽之東王彭城也，漢王還定三秦而東。

translations.literal: When Xiang Yu went east to enthrone himself at Pengcheng, the King of Han returned to settle the Three Qin and went east.
translations.idiomatic: Xiang Yu moved east to his capital at Pengcheng while the King of Han reconquered the Three Qin and marched eastward.

sentences.zh: 殷王反楚，項羽乃以平為信武君，將魏王客在楚者往擊，殷降而還。

translations.literal: The King of Yin rebelled against Chu; Xiang Yu then made Ping Lord Xinwu, leading the guests of the King of Wei who were in Chu to attack; Yin surrendered and they returned.
translations.idiomatic: When the King of Yin turned on Chu, Xiang Yu named Chen Ping Lord Xinwu and sent him with Wei retainers to crush the revolt; Yin submitted and they withdrew.

sentences.zh: 項王使項悍拜平為都尉，賜金二十溢。

translations.literal: King Xiang sent Xiang Han to invest Ping as commandant and gave twenty yi of gold.
translations.idiomatic: Xiang Yu had Xiang Han confirm him as commandant and showered him with twenty yi of gold.

sentences.zh: 居無何，漢攻下殷。

translations.literal: Before long, Han attacked and took Yin.
translations.idiomatic: Soon afterward Han forces captured Yin.

sentences.zh: 項王怒，將誅定殷者。

translations.literal: King Xiang was angry and was about to execute those who had settled Yin.
translations.idiomatic: Xiang Yu raged and prepared to execute everyone involved in the Yin campaign.

sentences.zh: 平懼誅，乃封其金與印，使使歸項王，而平身間行杖劍亡。

translations.literal: Ping feared execution; therefore he sealed the gold and seal, sent a messenger to return them to King Xiang, while Ping himself secretly went staff-sword in hand and fled.
translations.idiomatic: Chen Ping feared the headsman's axe: he boxed up his gold and seal, sent them back to Xiang Yu by courier, and fled alone with nothing but his sword.

sentences.zh: 度河，船人見其美丈夫，獨行，疑其亡將，要下當有寶器金玉，目之，欲殺平。

translations.literal: Crossing the river, the boatman saw that he was a handsome man traveling alone, suspected he was a fleeing general, and that beneath his waist there must be precious vessels of gold and jade; he eyed him and wished to kill Ping.
translations.idiomatic: At the crossing the ferryman saw a lone handsome traveler, guessed he was a routed commander laden with loot, and meant to rob and kill him.

sentences.zh: 平心恐，乃解衣臝而佐刺船。

translations.literal: Ping's heart feared; therefore he stripped his clothes bare and helped pole the boat.
translations.idiomatic: Chen Ping stripped to the skin and took up an oar to help row, showing he had nothing to steal.

sentences.zh: 船人知其無有，乃止。

translations.literal: The boatman knew he had nothing and then stopped.
translations.idiomatic: Seeing he owned nothing, the boatman let him be.

sentences.zh: 平遂至脩武降漢，因魏無知求見漢王，漢王召入。

translations.literal: Ping then reached Xiuwu and surrendered to Han; through Wei Wuzhi he sought audience with the King of Han; the King of Han summoned him in.
translations.idiomatic: He made his way to Xiuwu, surrendered to Han, and through Wei Wuzhi gained an audience with the King of Han.

sentences.zh: 是時，萬石君石奮為中涓，受平謁。

translations.literal: At that time the Lord of Ten Thousand Bushels, Shi Fen, was palace attendant and received Ping's petition.
translations.idiomatic: Shi Fen, the future "Lord of Ten Thousand Bushels," was then a palace usher and took his card.

sentences.zh: 平等十人俱進，賜食。

translations.literal: Ping and ten others together advanced and were granted food.
translations.idiomatic: Chen Ping and nine other newcomers were fed together.

sentences.zh: 王曰：「罷，就舍矣。」

translations.literal: The king said, "Enough; go to your lodgings."
translations.idiomatic: The king said, "That will do—go to your quarters."

sentences.zh: 平曰：「臣為事來，所言不可以過今日。」

translations.literal: Ping said, "Your servant came on business; what is spoken cannot pass beyond today."
translations.idiomatic: Chen Ping replied, "I came on urgent business; if it waits until tomorrow, it is too late."

sentences.zh: 於是漢王與語而說之，問曰：「子居楚何官？」

translations.literal: Thereupon the King of Han spoke with him and was pleased with him, asking, "What office did you hold in Chu?"
translations.idiomatic: The king talked with him, was won over, and asked what rank he had held under Xiang Yu.

sentences.zh: 平曰：「為都尉。」

translations.literal: Ping said, "I was commandant."
translations.idiomatic: He said he had been a commandant.

sentences.zh: 是日拜平為都尉，使參乘，典護軍。

translations.literal: That day he invested Ping as commandant, made him chariot companion, and put him in charge of guarding the army.
translations.idiomatic: The same day the king named him commandant, seated him as guard on the royal chariot, and put him over army security.

sentences.zh: 諸將盡讙，曰：「大王一日得楚之亡卒，未知高下，而即與共載，使監護長者！」

translations.literal: The generals all clamored, saying, "The great king in one day obtained Chu's fugitive soldier, not yet knowing high or low, and immediately shares a carriage with him and makes him oversee the elders!"
translations.idiomatic: The generals muttered that their lord had picked up some Chu deserter, knew nothing of his worth, yet shared a chariot with him and set him above veteran commanders.

sentences.zh: 漢王聞之，愈益幸平，遂與東伐項王。

translations.literal: When the King of Han heard it, he favored Ping all the more and therefore went east with him to attack King Xiang.
translations.idiomatic: The king only prized him the more and took him east against Xiang Yu.

sentences.zh: 至彭城，為楚所敗，引師而還。

translations.literal: When they reached Pengcheng, they were defeated by Chu; he led the army back.
translations.idiomatic: At Pengcheng Chu shattered the Han army and drove it back.

sentences.zh: 收散兵至滎陽，以平為亞將，屬韓王信，軍廣武。

translations.literal: Gathering scattered troops to Yingyang, he made Ping sub-general, subordinate to King Xin of Han, and encamped at Guangwu.
translations.idiomatic: Chen Ping rallied the fugitives at Yingyang; the king made him lieutenant-general under King Xin of Han and stationed the line at Guangwu.

sentences.zh: 絳、灌等或讒平曰：「平雖美丈夫，如冠玉耳，其中未必有也。

translations.literal: Zhou Bo of Jiang and Guan Ying and others slandered Ping, saying, "Although Ping is a handsome man, he is like a jade cap-ornament; inside there may not be anything.
translations.idiomatic: Marquis Jiang Zhou Bo and Guan Ying whispered that Chen Ping was pretty as polished jade and probably hollow within.

sentences.zh: 聞平居家時盜其嫂；

translations.literal: It is heard that when Ping dwelt at home he stole his sister-in-law;
translations.idiomatic: They claimed he had seduced his sister-in-law at home;

sentences.zh: 事魏王不容，亡而歸楚；

translations.literal: serving the King of Wei he was not tolerated, fled and returned to Chu;
translations.idiomatic: Wei Jiu would not keep him, so he fled to Chu;

sentences.zh: 歸楚不中，又亡歸漢。

translations.literal: returning to Chu he did not fit, again fled and returned to Han.
translations.idiomatic: Chu did not suit him either, so he ran to Han.

sentences.zh: 今大王尊官之，令護軍。

translations.literal: Now the great king honors him with office and orders him to guard the army.
translations.idiomatic: Now you give him high rank and the keys to your camp.

sentences.zh: 臣聞平使諸將，金多者得善處，金少者得惡處。

translations.literal: Your servant has heard that when Ping sends the generals, those with much gold get good posts and those with little gold get bad posts.
translations.idiomatic: They say he sells appointments—gold buys a soft billet, poverty earns the front line.

sentences.zh: 平，反覆亂臣也，願王察之。」

translations.literal: Ping is a turncoat disruptive minister; I beg the king to examine him."
translations.idiomatic: Chen Ping is a time-server, sire—look closely before you trust him."

sentences.zh: 漢王疑之，以讓無知，問曰：「有之乎？」

translations.literal: The King of Han suspected him and rebuked Wuzhi, asking, "Is it so?"
translations.idiomatic: The king summoned Wei Wuzhi and demanded whether the tales were true.

sentences.zh: 無知曰：「有。」

translations.literal: Wuzhi said, "It is so."
translations.idiomatic: Wei Wuzhi admitted they were.

sentences.zh: 漢王曰：「

translations.literal: The King of Han said, "
translations.idiomatic: The king said, "

sentences.zh: 公言其賢人何也？」

translations.literal: You called him a worthy man—why?"
translations.idiomatic: You praised him as a worthy—why?"

sentences.zh: 對曰：「臣之所言者，能也；

translations.literal: He answered, "What your servant spoke of is ability;
translations.idiomatic: Wei Wuzhi replied, "I spoke of his talent;

sentences.zh: 陛下所問者，行也。

translations.literal: what Your Majesty asked about is conduct.
translations.idiomatic: you ask about his morals.

sentences.zh: 今有尾生、孝已之行，而無益於勝敗之數，陛下何暇用之乎？

translations.literal: Now suppose there were the conduct of Weisheng and Xiaoji yet no benefit to the tally of victory and defeat—would Your Majesty have leisure to use them?
translations.idiomatic: Suppose a man had the constancy of Weisheng and the filial piety of Xiaoji yet could not tip the scales of war—would you have time for him?

sentences.zh: 今楚漢相距，臣進奇謀之士，顧其計誠足以利國家耳。

translations.literal: Now Chu and Han stand opposed; your servant advances a man of strange plans, looking only to whether his schemes truly suffice to benefit the state.
translations.idiomatic: Chu and Han are locked in stalemate; I recommend a strategist whose schemes must help the realm.

sentences.zh: 盜嫂受金又安足疑乎？」

translations.literal: As for stealing a sister-in-law and receiving gold—how is that enough to doubt?"
translations.idiomatic: Gossip about his sister-in-law or bribes is beside the point."

sentences.zh: 漢王召平而問曰：「吾聞先生事魏不遂，事楚而去，今又從吾游，信者固多心乎？」

translations.literal: The King of Han summoned Ping and asked, "I have heard that you, sir, served Wei without success, served Chu and left, and now again follow me on my travels—are the trustworthy indeed many-minded?"
translations.idiomatic: The king then called Chen Ping in and said, "You failed Wei, quit Chu, and now follow me—is loyalty so changeable?"

sentences.zh: 平曰：「臣事魏王，魏王不能用臣說，故去事項王。

translations.literal: Ping said, "Your servant served the King of Wei; the King of Wei could not use your servant's proposals; therefore I left to serve King Xiang.
translations.idiomatic: Chen Ping answered, "Wei Jiu would not heed my advice, so I went to Xiang Yu.

sentences.zh: 項王不信人，其所任愛，非諸項即妻之昆弟，雖有奇士不能用。

translations.literal: King Xiang does not trust people; those whom he favors and loves are not various Xiangs then wives' brothers; though there are strange scholars he cannot use them.
translations.idiomatic: Xiang Yu trusts only his clan and his wife's kin; no outsider, however brilliant, gets a hearing.

sentences.zh: 臣居楚聞漢王之能用人，故歸大王。

translations.literal: Your servant, dwelling in Chu, heard that the King of Han could employ people; therefore I returned to the great king.
translations.idiomatic: I heard that you know how to use men, and crossed to your side.

sentences.zh: 臝身來，不受金無以為資。

translations.literal: I came naked body; without receiving gold there was no means for capital.
translations.idiomatic: I arrived empty-handed; without your gold I could not pay messengers or agents.

sentences.zh: 誠臣計畫有可采者，願大王用之；

translations.literal: If truly your servant's plans have something worth taking, I beg the great king to use them;
translations.idiomatic: If any counsel of mine is sound, use it;

sentences.zh: 使無可用者，大王所賜金具在，請封輸官，得請骸骨。」

translations.literal: If there is nothing usable, the gold the great king granted is all present; I beg to seal and deliver it to the officials and request my bones."
translations.idiomatic: If not, every coin you gave lies sealed in its chest—take it back for the treasury and take my head as well."

sentences.zh: 漢王乃謝，厚賜，拜以為護軍中尉，盡護諸將。

translations.literal: The King of Han then apologized, richly rewarded him, invested him as commandant-in-chief of the guard, and had him oversee all the generals.
translations.idiomatic: The king apologized, heaped more gold on him, named him chief commandant of the guard, and set him over every captain.

sentences.zh: 諸將乃不敢復言。

translations.literal: The generals then dared not speak again.
translations.idiomatic: The murmurs died away.

sentences.zh: 其後，楚急擊，絕漢甬道，圍漢王於滎陽城。

translations.literal: Afterward Chu urgently attacked, cut off Han's supply corridor, and besieged the King of Han in Yingyang city.
translations.idiomatic: Later Chu stormed the supply road to Yingyang and penned the King of Han inside the walls.

sentences.zh: 漢王患之，請割滎陽以西和。

translations.literal: The King of Han worried over it and requested to cede the area west of Yingyang for peace.
translations.idiomatic: The king offered to yield everything west of Yingyang for a truce.

sentences.zh: 項王弗聽。

translations.literal: King Xiang would not listen.
translations.idiomatic: Xiang Yu refused.

sentences.zh: 漢王謂平曰：「天下紛紛，何時定乎？」

translations.literal: The King of Han said to Ping, "The realm is in turmoil; when will it be settled?"
